title: A Doll's House

passage.title: A Doll’s House (excerpt)
passage.author: By Henrik Ibsen
passage.text:
**Henrik Ibsen (1828–1906) was a Norwegian playwright. *A Doll’s House* focuses on the relationship between Nora Helmer and her husband, a bank manager. Nils Krogstad, an employee at the bank, is about to be fired. In this scene, Krogstad attempts to blackmail Nora.** 

1. KROGSTAD: When your husband was ill, you came to me to borrow two hundred and fifty pounds.

2. NORA: I didn’t know anyone else to go to.

3. KROGSTAD: I promised to get you that amount—

4. NORA: Yes, and you did so.

5. KROGSTAD: I promised to get you that amount, on certain conditions. Your mind was so taken up with your husband’s illness, and you were so anxious to get the money for your journey, that you seem to have paid no attention to the conditions of our bargain. Therefore it will not be [word] if I remind you of them. Now, I promised to get the money on the security of a [word] which I drew up.

6. NORA: Yes, and which I signed.

7. KROGSTAD: Good. But below your signature there were a few lines making your father a [word] for the money; those lines your father should have signed.

8. NORA: Should? He did sign them.

9. KROGSTAD: I had left the date blank; that is to say, your father should himself have inserted the date on which he signed the paper. Do you remember that?

10. NORA: Yes, I think I remember—

11. KROGSTAD: Then I gave you the bond to send by post to your father. Is that not so?

12. NORA: Yes.

13. KROGSTAD: And you naturally did so at once, because five or six days afterwards you brought me the bond with your father’s signature. And then I gave you the money.

14. NORA: Well, haven’t I been paying it off regularly?

15. KROGSTAD: Fairly so, yes. But—to come back to the matter in hand—that must have been a very __trying__ time for you, Mrs. Helmer?

16. NORA: It was, indeed.

17. KROGSTAD: Your father was very ill, wasn’t he?

18. NORA: He was very near his end.

19. KROGSTAD: And died soon afterwards?

20. NORA: Yes.

21. KROGSTAD: Tell me, Mrs. Helmer, can you by any chance remember what day your father died?—on what day of the month, I mean.

22. NORA: Papa died on the 29th of September.

23. KROGSTAD: That is correct; I have [word] it for myself. And, as that is so, there is a [word] [*taking a paper from his pocket*] which I cannot account for.

24. NORA: What discrepancy? I don’t know—

25. KROGSTAD: The discrepancy consists, Mrs Helmer, in the fact that your father signed this bond three days after his death.

26. NORA: What do you mean? I don’t understand—

27. KROGSTAD: Your father died on the 29th of September. But, look here; your father has dated his signature the 2nd of October. It is a discrepancy, isn’t it? [*NORA is silent.*] Can you explain it to me? [*NORA is still silent.*] It is a remarkable thing, too, that the words “2nd of October,” as well as the year, are not written in your father’s handwriting but in one that I think I know. Well, of course it can be explained; your father may have forgotten to date his signature, and someone else may have dated it [word] before they knew of his death. There is no harm in that. It all depends on the signature of the name; and that is genuine, I suppose, Mrs .Helmer? It was your father himself who signed his name here?

28. NORA: [*after a short pause, throws her head up and looks defiantly at him*]. No, it was not. It was I that wrote papa’s name.

29. KROGSTAD: Are you aware that is a dangerous confession?

30. NORA: In what way? You shall have your money soon.

31. KROGSTAD: Let me ask you a question; why did you not send the paper to your father?

32. NORA: It was impossible; papa was so ill. If I had asked him for his signature, I should have had to tell him what the money was to be used for; and when he was so ill himself I couldn’t tell him that my husband’s life was in danger—it was impossible.

33. KROGSTAD: It would have been better for you if you had given up your trip abroad.

34. NORA: No, that was impossible. That trip was to save my husband’s life; I couldn’t give that up.

35. KROGSTAD: But did it never occur to you that you were committing a fraud on me?

36. NORA: I couldn’t take that into account; I didn’t trouble myself about you at all. I couldn’t bear you, because you put so many heartless difficulties in my way, although you knew what a dangerous condition my husband was in.

37. KROGSTAD: Mrs. Helmer, you evidently do not realize clearly what it is that you have been guilty of. But I can assure you that my one false step, which lost me all my reputation, was nothing more or nothing worse than what you have done.

38. NORA: You? Do you ask me to believe that you were brave enough to run a risk to save your wife’s life?

39. KROGSTAD: The law cares nothing about [word].

40. NORA: Then it must be a very foolish law.

41. KROGSTAD: Foolish or not, it is the law by which you will be judged, if I produce this paper in court.

42. NORA: I don’t believe it. Is a daughter not to be allowed to spare her dying father anxiety and care? Is a wife not to be allowed to save her husband’s life? I don’t know much about law; but I am certain that there must be laws permitting such things as that. Have you no knowledge of such laws—you who are a lawyer? You must be a very poor lawyer, Mr. Krogstad.

43. KROGSTAD: Maybe. But matters of business—such business as you and I have had together—do you think I don’t understand that? Very well. Do as you please. But let me tell you this—if I lose my position a second time, you shall lose yours with me. [*He bows, and goes out through the hall.*]

questions.question_content: **Which inference is best supported by the text?**

choices.label: A
choices.content: Krogstad wants Nora to pay back the money he loaned her immediately because he suspects he will be fired from the bank.

choices.label: B
choices.content: Nora knew she was breaking the law when she signed the loan papers, but she was sure she wouldn’t get caught.

choices.label: C
choices.content: Krogstad knew that Nora had committed fraud before he began questioning her about the loan he gave her.

choices.label: D
choices.content: Nora’s husband is already aware that she forged the loan papers, so Krogstad’s blackmail will be ineffective.

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
